reviews.summary: This paper presents a fixed-bank benchmark studying whether compatibility-based weighting improves causal discovery when aggregating local CPDAGs learned on overlapping observed-variable subsets under marginalization error. The main finding is negative: compatibility weighting produces only a 0.0286 median reduction in false-orientation rate over uniform weighting, failing to meet the pre-registered 10% threshold. The paper emphasizes that subset design choices matter more than the weighting heuristic.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed 6 distinct online searches: (1) exact title search found no matches, (2) 'compatibility weighting causal discovery subset aggregation' found related but distinct work, (3) verified Self-Compatibility (Faller et al. 2024) exists and is properly cited, (4) verified SEA (Wu et al. 2025) exists, (5) verified consistency-guided aggregation (Ninad et al. 2025) exists, (6) verified Intersection-Validation (Viinikka et al. 2018) and DAGBag (Wang & Peng 2014) exist. The paper is positioned as a benchmark study with a negative result rather than a new method. The key related works (SEA, Self-Compatibility, consistency-guided aggregation) substantially narrow the novelty of any new subset-aggregation method. The paper acknowledges this and frames its contribution as 'empirical and narrower'—a controlled benchmark isolating weighting and merging decisions. While the specific fixed-bank benchmark design is new, the core ideas (compatibility weighting, subset aggregation) are not. The negative result is useful but incremental.
strengths: Excellent experimental design with pre-registered success criteria (10% false-orientation reduction threshold)
Comprehensive subset-validity audit showing direction validity is only 0.110-0.158, providing insight into why compatibility weighting fails
Fixed-bank design properly isolates the contribution of compatibility weighting from confounds
Strong reproducibility: all hyperparameters, seeds, and compute details specified; CPU-only execution
Thorough ablations showing subset size (k=6,8,10) matters more than weighting choice
Honest reporting of negative results with proper statistical tests (Wilcoxon p=0.351)
Complete experiment code available and well-organized
Results in paper match results.json exactly
weaknesses: The novelty is limited: Self-Compatibility (Faller et al. 2024), SEA (Wu et al. 2025), and consistency-guided aggregation (Ninad et al. 2025) cover similar ground
Benchmark limited to p=20 variables—very small scale for modern causal discovery
Only PC-Stable used for local learning; conclusions may not generalize to other local learners
Main hypothesis was not supported, making the paper's contribution primarily negative
No real data experiments (acknowledged as limitation but still a weakness)
LOVO baseline was skipped due to 'operational ambiguity'—weakens comparison to related work
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is well-executed but needs to better articulate its unique contribution given related work. The authors should: (1) Expand the discussion of how this benchmark differs fundamentally from SEA (which uses learned aggregation modules) and consistency-guided aggregation (which studies vector-valued variables). The current positioning is somewhat defensive. (2) Consider adding at least one experiment with a different local learner (e.g., GES on subsets) to strengthen generalizability claims. (3) The LOVO baseline should either be implemented or the comparison should be de-emphasized—skipping a baseline 'due to operational ambiguity' is unsatisfying. (4) Given that full-graph baselines (GES, PC, DAGBag-PC) dominate all subset methods, the paper should more directly address when (if ever) subset aggregation is preferable. (5) The scale limitation (p=20) should be discussed more prominently; many real applications have hundreds or thousands of variables.
questions_for_authors: How does your fixed-bank benchmark relate methodologically to the SEA framework (Wu et al. 2025)? Could SEA's learned aggregation be seen as a solution to the limitations you identify?
Given that full-graph baselines are much stronger than subset methods in your benchmark, under what conditions would you recommend subset aggregation at all?
Could the poor performance of compatibility weighting be specific to PC-Stable as the local learner? Have you tested with other local discovery algorithms?
Why was LOVO-Select not implementable? The paper mentions 'operational ambiguity' but this is vague—please clarify or implement the baseline.
reviews.integrity_check: All results appear genuine. The experiment code exists and implements exactly what the paper describes. The results.json file contains 48 datasets with metrics that match the paper's tables (e.g., GES mean SHD 6.83, CompatExp+DetThreshold mean SHD 18.52). The logs show actual training runs with proper epoch/loss tracking. All figures are generated from the actual results. All citations I verified (Faller et al. 2024, Wu et al. 2025, Ninad et al. 2025, Viinikka et al. 2018, Wang & Peng 2014, Colombo & Maathuis 2014, Chickering 2002, Zheng et al. 2018) are real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues. No evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a fixed-bank benchmark for evaluating compatibility-weighted subset aggregation in classical causal discovery under marginalization error. Across 48 synthetic datasets, the authors show that compatibility weighting yields negligible improvement over uniform weighting (0.0286 median false-orientation rate reduction, p=0.351), while full-graph baselines like GES (SHD 6.83) vastly outperform all subset methods (SHD ~18.5). The main conclusion is negative: subset compatibility cannot overcome the severe orientation distortion caused by marginalization.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Six online searches were conducted. The exact paper title does not appear in any search results, confirming it is not previously published. However, the core ideas are assembled from existing work with limited novel contribution. Self-Compatibility (Faller et al., AISTATS 2024) already introduced the idea of using subset compatibility for evaluating causal discovery. SEA (Wu et al., TMLR 2025) already studied subset-estimate-aggregate pipelines. Ninad et al. (2025) already analyzed consistency-guided aggregation with explicit failure mode analysis. The LOVO work (Schkoda et al., CLeaR 2025) extends internal validation via variable subsets. This paper's contribution is narrower — a benchmark that fixes the local evidence bank and varies only weighting/merging — but the negative finding that marginalization distortion undermines subset aggregation is somewhat expected given the theoretical arguments already present in Ninad et al. The subset-validity audit (Table 1) is the most original diagnostic contribution, but it does not constitute a methodological advance.
strengths: Honest and well-framed negative result: the paper clearly states its pre-registered success criterion (0.10 hard-regime FOR reduction) and honestly reports falling far short (0.0286, p=0.351)
The fixed-bank experimental design is methodologically clean — all subset methods share identical local evidence, isolating the contribution of weighting and merging decisions

The subset-validity audit (Table 1) is a useful diagnostic that quantifies claim-family-level distortion before interpreting aggregation gains, showing the asymmetry between reliable non-adjacency claims (0.98-0.99) and unreliable direction claims (0.11-0.16)
Thorough ablations covering alternative weights, claim families, contradiction definitions, and subset sizes, with the subset-size finding (k=10 vs k=8 vs k=6) being the most actionable result
Good reproducibility: all seeds, hyperparameters, and software versions are specified; experiment source code is present in the workspace
weaknesses: Low novelty: the paper does not propose a new method, algorithm, or theoretical result. The benchmark combines existing ideas (compatibility from Faller 2024, subset aggregation from Wu 2025, bootstrap stability) and the negative finding is not surprising given known theoretical issues with marginalization in causal graphs

NOTEARS-L1 produces completely empty graphs across all 48 datasets (skeleton F1=0.0, graph density=0.0, all orientation metrics=0.0), yet this is listed in Table 2 without any discussion. This suggests either a serious implementation bug or severely mistuned hyperparameters, undermining confidence in the baseline comparisons

Very narrow scope: only p=20 variables, only synthetic data, only PC-Stable as the local learner, only one subset construction strategy. The authors acknowledge these limitations but the resulting conclusions may not generalize

Limited significance: the negative conclusion — that compatibility weighting doesn't help when local evidence is severely distorted by marginalization — is arguably the expected outcome given the theoretical analysis in Ninad et al. (2025) and the known non-closure of CPDAGs under marginalization

The paper lacks theoretical analysis explaining why compatibility weighting fails. A formal argument connecting marginalization distortion to the ineffectiveness of reweighting would substantially strengthen the contribution

Uniform+Wilson and Uniform+DetThreshold produce identical results across all metrics (Table 2), suggesting the Wilson confidence bound adds nothing over simple thresholding in this setting, but this is not discussed
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would benefit from several improvements to reach publication quality. First, the NOTEARS-L1 anomaly must be addressed — a baseline that produces empty graphs across all datasets either indicates a bug or requires explicit discussion of why the L1 penalty eliminates all edges at this scale. Second, the paper needs a theoretical or at least intuitive argument for why compatibility weighting is specifically ineffective under marginalization, beyond the empirical observation. The subset-validity audit is the strongest contribution; the authors should consider framing the paper more around this diagnostic tool and its implications for future subset aggregation work. Third, the benchmark scope should be expanded: testing with at least one additional local learner (e.g., GES-based subsets), larger variable counts (p=50 or 100), and ideally a principled subset construction strategy would make the negative result more convincing and the benchmark more useful. Fourth, the finding that Uniform+Wilson equals Uniform+DetThreshold identically deserves discussion — it reveals that the Wilson screen's additional machinery provides no benefit here. Finally, the paper should more explicitly discuss what positive contribution it makes beyond 'this doesn't work': the subset-validity audit methodology and the subset-size dominance finding are both useful, but they are somewhat buried in the current framing.
questions_for_authors: Why does NOTEARS-L1 produce completely empty graphs (F1=0.0, density=0.0) across all 48 datasets? Was this investigated, and if so, what was the cause?

Have you tested with any local learner other than PC-Stable? The negative result might be specific to constraint-based methods, which are known to be sensitive to marginalization, while score-based local learners might behave differently.
Why are Uniform+Wilson and Uniform+DetThreshold identical across all metrics? Does this imply the Wilson bound is always above or below 0.5 whenever the threshold is above or below 0.6?

Have you considered testing at larger variable counts (p=50 or p=100)? The marginalization pressure should change with problem scale, potentially altering the relative effectiveness of compatibility weighting.
Could you provide a theoretical argument (even informal) for why the compatibility score C_g cannot distinguish marginalization-induced contradictions from finite-sample contradictions?
reviews.integrity_check: Experiment source code exists in exp/ with proper modular structure (shared/, environment/, data/, local_bank/, baselines/, main/, ablations/, evaluation/). All numbers in Table 2 match results.json exactly for both main methods and baselines. The p-values and effect sizes in the pre-registered test (p=0.351, median difference 0.0286) match results.json. Figures are genuine generated plots consistent with the reported data. Logs show actual execution traces from the Codex automation system. One concern: NOTEARS-L1 produces entirely empty graphs (all structural metrics are exactly 0.0) across all 48 datasets, which is abnormal and unremarked in the paper — this raises questions about the fidelity of that baseline implementation. Reference integrity is good: all 13 cited papers were verified to exist with correct titles, authors, and venues via web search.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a fixed-bank benchmark of subset-estimate-aggregate causal discovery under marginalization error rather than a new discovery algorithm. It keeps a bank of local PC-Stable subset CPDAGs fixed across 48 synthetic datasets, audits how those local claims deviate from induced-subgraph truth, and compares compatibility-based weighting against uniform and bootstrap-stability weighting when merging to a global graph.
reviews.novelty_assessment: An exact-title search did not reveal a prior version of this paper. However, the conceptual space is already crowded by close prior work: Self-Compatibility studies subset disagreement as an internal falsification/model-selection signal; LOVO extends variable-subset cross-validation; SEA explicitly follows a sample/estimate/aggregate recipe for causal discovery; and Ninad et al. (2025) study consistency-guided aggregation and failure modes when aggregation is structurally invalid. I also found additional nearby work on overlapping or non-identical variable sets, including CLeaR 2025 papers on overlapping datasets and the very recent I-CAM-UV paper on integrating causal graphs over non-identical variable sets. Given this landscape, the contribution here is best understood as a careful empirical benchmark with a negative result, not a novel method. That is still potentially publishable, but the novelty is limited and the related-work positioning is not yet strong enough.
strengths: The paper is commendably honest about a negative result and uses a pre-registered success criterion rather than retrofitting a positive story.

The fixed-bank design is scientifically sensible: by holding the local subset evidence fixed, the study isolates weighting and merge-rule effects instead of conflating them with subset sampling noise.
The subset-validity audit is useful and informative. Quantifying that non-adjacency claims remain reliable while direction and collider claims are heavily distorted is a concrete insight.

The artifact package is substantial: source code is present, the benchmark pipeline is implemented, experiment-stage logs exist, and the reported paper numbers match the saved aggregate results up to normal rounding.
The paper is generally well written, and the main claim is easy to understand: compatibility weighting does not rescue subset aggregation once marginalization error dominates the local evidence.
weaknesses: Novelty is limited. Most core ingredients already exist in nearby literature, and the paper's distinction from that literature is narrower than the framing initially suggests.

The related-work coverage is incomplete for such a novelty-sensitive paper. In particular, recent work on overlapping datasets/non-identical variable sets is highly relevant and should be discussed directly.
The experimental scope is narrow for the breadth of the conclusion: all subset methods use one local learner family, one main subset-bank construction, and only synthetic data with p=20.

Experimental rigor is decent but not strong enough for an acceptance recommendation: there are many baselines and ablations, but uncertainty reporting is limited, and the subset-size study is restricted to the hard-regime slice.
A planned LOVO baseline was not run, which leaves one of the closest comparison points missing.

Reference integrity is mostly fine, but not perfect: for example, the cited LOVO paper is real, yet the bibliography metadata in the workspace uses an author order that does not match the official PMLR record.
reviews.detailed_feedback: I would encourage the authors to lean fully into the benchmark-paper identity and tighten the scope of the claims. The paper becomes stronger if it explicitly states that the contribution is a controlled benchmark/audit of subset aggregation under marginalization error, rather than any new aggregation principle. To improve the paper, I would add a fuller comparison to recent overlapping-dataset and graph-integration work, discuss more carefully why induced-subgraph truth is the right audit target for the stated question, and broaden the sensitivity analysis beyond the current single local learner and single main subset-bank construction. The paper would also benefit from stronger uncertainty reporting in the main tables, a clearer discussion of generalization limits beyond PC-based local learners, and a more careful explanation of which conclusions are benchmark-specific versus likely to transfer. Finally, the citation list should be cleaned against official records and the missing LOVO comparison should either be implemented or more convincingly justified as unnecessary.
questions_for_authors: How sensitive are the main conclusions to the subset-bank seed and bank-construction heuristic, not just to subset size k?
Why is the audit performed only against induced-subgraph CPDAG truth rather than against a marginalization-aware target, and how should readers interpret that choice?
Do the negative conclusions persist with a different local learner family, or is the result mainly about PC-Stable-based local graphs?
Can you position the paper more explicitly against recent work on overlapping/non-identical variable-set causal graph integration?
Can you correct the bibliography metadata, especially for the LOVO citation, to match the official publication record?
reviews.integrity_check: Results appear broadly genuine. The workspace contains the experiment source code, per-method result files, generated figures, and benchmark-stage logs showing dataset generation, local-bank construction, baseline runs, subset-method runs, ablations, and evaluation on 2026-03-23. I cross-checked the paper's reported table values and main numerical claims against results.json/tables and they match up to rounding. One minor artifact issue exists: some saved local-bank per-dataset runtime fields are zero due to how timing metadata is recorded, but this does not appear to affect the paper's reported structural results.

aggregated_feedback:
--- Review 1 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 6) ---
  Weaknesses: The novelty is limited: Self-Compatibility (Faller et al. 2024), SEA (Wu et al. 2025), and consistency-guided aggregation (Ninad et al. 2025) cover similar ground
Benchmark limited to p=20 variables—very small scale for modern causal discovery
Only PC-Stable used for local learning; conclusions may not generalize to other local learners
Main hypothesis was not supported, making the paper's contribution primarily negative
No real data experiments (acknowledged as limitation but still a weakness)
LOVO baseline was skipped due to 'operational ambiguity'—weakens comparison to related work
  Feedback: The paper is well-executed but needs to better articulate its unique contribution given related work. The authors should: (1) Expand the discussion of how this benchmark differs fundamentally from SEA (which uses learned aggregation modules) and consistency-guided aggregation (which studies vector-valued variables). The current positioning is somewhat defensive. (2) Consider adding at least one experiment with a different local learner (e.g., GES on subsets) to strengthen generalizability claims. (3) The LOVO baseline should either be implemented or the comparison should be de-emphasized—skipping a baseline 'due to operational ambiguity' is unsatisfying. (4) Given that full-graph baselines (GES, PC, DAGBag-PC) dominate all subset methods, the paper should more directly address when (if ever) subset aggregation is preferable. (5) The scale limitation (p=20) should be discussed more prominently; many real applications have hundreds or thousands of variables.
  Integrity: All results appear genuine. The experiment code exists and implements exactly what the paper describes. The results.json file contains 48 datasets with metrics that match the paper's tables (e.g., GES mean SHD 6.83, CompatExp+DetThreshold mean SHD 18.52). The logs show actual training runs with proper epoch/loss tracking. All figures are generated from the actual results. All citations I verified (Faller et al. 2024, Wu et al. 2025, Ninad et al. 2025, Viinikka et al. 2018, Wang & Peng 2014, Colombo & Maathuis 2014, Chickering 2002, Zheng et al. 2018) are real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues. No evidence of fabrication or misconduct.
